name: I got exposed to chemicals at work and I'm feeling sick now—what do I do first without getting fired?
description: Seek medical care immediately and notify your employer in writing about the exposure. Document the specific chemicals involved by requesting Safety Data Sheets

mainEntity.name: I got exposed to chemicals at work and I'm feeling sick now—what do I do first without getting fired?

acceptedAnswer.text: Seek medical care immediately and notify your employer in writing about the exposure. Document the specific chemicals involved by requesting Safety Data Sheets (SDS) and keep a record of your symptoms to ensure your claim is protected under Utah's reporting deadlines.
acceptedAnswer.description:
If you suspect exposure, your health and your legal rights depend on immediate action. First, seek medical attention from a provider who understands occupational medicine and tell them exactly what chemicals you were exposed to. Second, notify your supervisor in writing—even if they already know—to create a paper trail. In Utah, failing to report an 'accident' or exposure event within 180 days can jeopardize your entire claim.

Immediate steps should include:
- Getting a copy of the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) for the chemicals involved.
- Taking photos of the area where the exposure occurred.
- Writing down a detailed timeline of your symptoms and the event.

Do not sign any statements from insurance adjusters before speaking with a professional. Early documentation is the best way to prevent an employer from later claiming the exposure happened elsewhere. Having a lawyer involved early ensures your report is phrased in a way that protects your rights.
